summary: I remember that after Pearl Harbor happened, my father was part of a young men's group in Hanford that included both Caucasians and Japanese Americans. Because he taught Japanese language on weekends, a friend warned him that the FBI might come after him. This was a tense time with curfews and fear, but fortunately the FBI never came for my father.

Hanford was where my parents lived and where my father taught Japanese language before the war. It’s also where my mother was born and where my parents met in high school. My father’s family lived nearby in Venice, Los Angeles, but Hanford was home for us before we were evacuated.

After Pearl Harbor, there was a real fear that the FBI would come for my father because he taught Japanese language. My father even packed a small suitcase in case they came, and we heard that some friends had been taken by the FBI. Thankfully, they never came for him, but the threat was always there.

My family was evacuated and sent to internment camps after Pearl Harbor. I was very young, so I remember camp as a kind of big gathering with playmates and activities like Brownies and Scouts. My parents were fearful and anxious, but they were open about their experiences later, and my father captured much of camp life in his paintings.

After leaving the camps in Arkansas, my family moved to New York City because my father, an artist, wanted to be there. He had been there before and enjoyed the artistic community, so it was a family decision to relocate. In New York, my father continued painting and expressing his camp experiences through art.

We were sent to internment camps in Arkansas, first Jerome and then Rohwer. As a child, I remember exploring outside the barbed wire fences and playing with friends. My parents both taught in the camp schools, and my father continued painting using raw canvas scraps from our belongings.

There was a Boy Scout troop in the camp where we lived in Arkansas. Although I was only six, I remember that there were scouting activities available for children, which helped create a sense of normalcy. It was part of the community life we had despite being in internment.

In the camp, there was also a Girl Scout troop, and since I was six, I joined the Brownies, which is part of the Girl Scouts. These activities made camp life feel more like a community and helped me have a happy childhood despite the circumstances.

As a six-year-old in the internment camp, I joined the Brownies, which was part of the Girl Scouts. It was one of the ways children like me had activities and playmates, making the camp experience less frightening and more like a gathering.

Hanford was where my family lived before the war and where my father was involved in the community, teaching Japanese language. It was also where my mother was born and where my parents met in high school. Hanford was our home until we were evacuated to the camps.

My father was born in Wakayama Prefecture in Japan before coming to the United States. His parents had already moved to Venice, Los Angeles, and later he joined them there. He was not a U.S. citizen until later in life.

My father’s parents lived in Venice, Los Angeles, and that’s where he joined them after being born in Wakayama Prefecture. It was part of his early life before settling in Hanford and later being interned during the war.

Our family was first sent to the Jerome War Relocation Center in Arkansas after evacuation. I remember it as a place where we lived with other Japanese American families, attended school, and had some normal childhood activities. When Jerome closed, we were moved to Rohwer.

After Jerome closed, we were relocated to the Rohwer War Relocation Center, also in Arkansas. Life there continued with schooling and community activities, and my parents taught in the camp schools. Eventually, we left Rohwer to move to New York City.

My father painted a piece called Final Decision showing a Japanese American soldier looking at a picture of his parents with an American flag on the wall. It captures the irony of young men wanting to serve in the U.S. Army while still being held in camps. This painting reflects the complex feelings of loyalty and identity during that time.

Another painting by my father, Am I An American Citizen?, shows two men chopping wood, one with his head in his hands and the other consoling him. It expresses the confusion and ambivalence about being interned yet wanting to serve as American citizens. This work reflects the emotional conflict experienced by many in the camps.

My uncle Ralph, my father’s younger brother, served in the United States Army during World War II. He was part of the segregated 442nd Infantry Regiment and fought in Sicily and Italy. His service was significant because it showed loyalty despite the discrimination faced by Japanese Americans.

My uncle Ralph served in the 442nd Infantry Regiment, a segregated Japanese American unit in the U.S. Army. He fought in Italy and Sicily and was wounded by shrapnel. This regiment was notable for its bravery and for the complex position of Japanese Americans fighting for a country that had interned their families.

My uncle Ralph fought in Sicily as part of the 442nd Infantry Regiment during World War II. This was one of the European campaigns where Japanese American soldiers served with distinction. He was injured there but continued to talk about his experiences long after the war.

Italy was another major battlefield where my uncle Ralph served with the 442nd Infantry Regiment. Fighting there was part of the effort to keep Japanese American soldiers away from the Pacific theater. His experiences in Italy were significant and shaped his post-war reflections.

After moving to New York, I pursued a career in nursing and graduated from the Cornell New York Hospital School of Nursing. I also worked at Cornell New York Hospital, which was a significant part of my professional life. This was a new chapter after the upheaval of the war and internment.

Although not detailed extensively, I attended Teachers College at Columbia University, which complemented my professional development. This education helped me build a career after the war, following my family’s relocation to New York City.
The September 11 attacks were not mentioned in my story or experiences. My narrative focuses on World War II, internment, and my family’s life before and after that period.
